video_title: The $3.5 Trillion IPO Wave That Could Shake the Entire Market
transcript: This is is a unique summer for a couple of reasons. These are much larger companies than we're used to going public. When you look at the three combined, you know, they end up rivaling probably a $3.5 trillion type valuation. This is really going to impact the entire stock market because whether you're in IPO markets or not, that doesn't matter. >> Hello and welcome to Wealthy. I'm Maggie Lake. Joining me today to discuss the blockbuster IPOs that are slated for this summer and this year is Brett Rentmester, founder and managing director of Windrock Wealth Management. Hi Brett, great to see you again. >> Nice to see you. >> This is going to be a different kind of blockbuster summer I think that than we're used to discussing. Hi everybody tuning in. Uh just a reminder if you would like some advice about whether IPOs are the right fit for you or your asset allocation, you can get a free portfolio review from a member of the Windrock team or one of the adviserss in our network. Just hit the link or go to wealthon.comfree. Uh so Brett, I think what makes this so special and why we're all talking about this already is that these are some of the biggest names in technology expected to hit public markets this year. SpaceX subvers expected to be the first out of the gate but Anthropic Open AI are also expected to come to market. Have we ever seen this kind of lineup of such large companies coming back to back in the same year? >> Not quite like this Maggie. I mean this is is a unique summer for a couple of reasons. And before I dive in I I just have to again give the disclaimer that this is not investment advice. All of this is just educational only and that Windrock employees and clients do own a lot of these names. So this, you know, it's just going to be general in nature. Um, but those are the names coming out and that the market is talking about it. So let's talk about three reasons this is different than normal. I think reason number one is the IPO markets. People have to recognize they've changed dramatically over the years. So, a lot of us are still locked into almost the 2000 time frame when it was the dotcoms. And back then, the average company was kind of a new startup concept. Uh it was like a small cap stock, something you you weren't sure on the concept or the name. And now the average life of an IPO is 12 years. So, they're getting uh you know longerdated companies. Part of that's regulation, Sarbain, Oxley, and the cost of being public. Now, of the roster you mentioned, a lot of people don't realize SpaceX is 24 years old, believe it or not. Now, the AI companies, you know, depending what you come up with, they're they're shorter. But, you know, in general, these are much larger companies than we're used to going public. Um and uh you know the the other implication of that is because they already have valuations of large companies most of like the early stage appreciation and benefits have already acrewed to the private holders people in private markets that have held the private shares. So that's one difference. The second difference is because of the size um some if not all of these could end up in the top 10 of the S&P 500 depending on their ultimate IPO valuation. Um >> right now we know SpaceX just based on the numbers would be right up there. It's a little less certain with Anthropic and Open AAI. They're still off in the distance, but at current private market valuations, you know, they themselves might be really close, if not in the top 10. So when you look at the three combined, you know, they end up rivaling probably a $ three.5 trillion dollar type valuation based on estimates today, which would be about 6 and a.5% of the S&P 500. So the other different dynamic here is these are not only larger companies, they're going to be major components of the indexes etc. And the third point of the difference related to that is unlike a normal IPO market where maybe there's a name or two um that you pay attention to or not, this is really going to impact the entire stock market because whether you're in IPO markets or not, that doesn't matter. These are going to be major constituents of the stock market and investors are going to have to make a conscious decision whether to be in or out of these and and there's pros and cons for each. But um obviously with these high valuations, you know, a lot of these trillion dollar plus, there's a thinner margin of error. So those are some of the key differences out of the gate. >> Yeah, those are huge differences, too. And I think that it maybe and maybe we can talk break it up and talk about it this way because I think there are there are issues that are specific to the individual companies, not maybe the companies themselves, but sort of around the the initial public offering and then there are these massive sort of market implications. So when it comes to the event itself, I'm wondering how given what you just described, how bankers even going to go about pricing this. I mean, with the valuations we're talking about, does anybody have a sense? I mean, I'm guessing it's going to be based on the private market valuations, but are we kind of in uncharted territory here given the huge size of these companies now? Well, I mean, we've had other large companies come to market in IPOs. I mean, Google was probably the best example. That that was back in 2004. Facebook in 2012, which was large. So, we have had these kind of things. These are just a little bit bigger and these three companies you mentioned are, you know, large scale. So, you know, I think what's typical in an IPO process is there's something called a road show where they go out, the company does, and they meet with potential investors, big investment banks, and they kind of size up the supply and demand decision, which ultimately arrives at the pricing um for the first day of the IPO. So I think in that process pricing is set or you get >> but I'm wondering since so many people own it in the private market you know like if the dynamic is different because they are so widely held privately and they're so well known. I mean normally you sort of present your business case and figure out what the market appetite is and you get peppered with questions about your viability. Now, yeah, these are so well known. The road shows got to be and and and from what I've seen from the years I've been covering it, you expect an appreciation and a pop on that first day. People want to see that stock go up as a sign that it was a successful IPO. >> Yeah, that's right. And because of the size, Maggie, I mean, to your point, I was looking back at the Tesla IPO as just a example. You know, when that went public, it did not get included in the indexes. So, you know, oftentimes indexes take time to add things. So, it had no waiting and it had a $ 1.7 billion dollar valuation. So, it was like buying into a small to midsize company and and having the opportunity to be part of that during its growth cycle. Now, the the maybe excitement but risk at the same time is that these are better known companies, more scalable, but they're already at trillion dollar plus kind of valuations and more for SpaceX. So those early stage gains from smaller companies, you know, are again they've acred to the private shareholders. So it's a little bit of a different dynamic here. >> Yeah. And you have to understand that you're going in at that part of the cycle, you know, as opposed to sort of that early excitement. So let's talk a little bit about the the the risk profile and the opportunity set. So if you had, you know, someone roll up from our audience who's not already holding the stock, doesn't have has not gained any access to it and is excited about this IPO, you know, what kind of what kind of conversation do we need to be having with ourselves as investors when it comes to the sort of risk opportunity profile? What are the smart questions we should be asking ourselves? >> Yeah. Well, I think there a couple key just basic observations and that is when things go public, they start trading as stocks and it really just becomes a single stock purchase decision. Do you want to own this or do you want to own that? Just like, you know, do you want to own Microsoft or Apple or any of these other companies out there as examples, right? Um the reality is most retail investors will probably not get access much access if any to the IPO price. Therefore, it's just buying a stock. So, in most people's framework and most advisors, that's not an asset allocation. It doesn't fit in a framework. It's just a single stock purchase decision just like buying any other stock. So, I think basic rules of buying stocks apply. You know, what's your position size? You know, how much conviction do you have in the idea itself or the company itself? you know, what's your plan to take profit if it goes up or what are you going to do if it falls? I mean, I think the reality is IPOs have had a very mixed history. So, while we can think back to successes like Google, there have also been companies that still exist like Rivian and others that are way down and many that have gone away. So, it's not a very clear playbook as it relates to IPOs themselves. And we've never really viewed IPOs as their own thing. It's for us it's always been do you want to be in the private markets and maybe someday they go public and that's your liquidity or do you want to buy these public equities that trade on markets. The IPO is kind of that initial trigger of the race right when it's you know right when it's listed and unfortunately because of that it attracts a lot of speculation a lot of traders a lot of people that maybe have no intention of really being in it in the long term. So I think um there's a hurting mentality that gets the hyped into this. So I think there's got to be a lot of caution around the IPO day itself and there those first couple days for average investors. >> Absolutely. I remember talking to people about this um especially during some more sort of euphoric times. Um, and there were some folks who just wouldn't touch them as a publicly traded stock for the first few weeks until they could get a trading pattern and get a sense of what was going on. I just want to underscore something that you said that's really important uh because there's been some headlines um about retail participation. So, not everyone is going to get access to the IPO price. So, if you see a price print and this is super basic. I know a lot of you know this, but it's worth reminding people because normally no retail gets access and so everyone's in the same boat. It may be a little different this time. I've heard some talk that maybe they're going to try to have an allocation for retail. I seem to remember when Google went public, they experimented with a Dutch auction. The same thing like in an attempt to get um the smaller investor, >> but whether people can get their hands on it or not before it's trading is the question >> is another question. And so if you see that that Google priced at because it'll be massive headlines what the original price is which could be revised if I'm not mistaken as well um that doesn't mean that is the price that you are going to get and you know you will know the difference but make sure that you understand if you're getting that that initial allocation through a special um program they've set up or if you're buying it on the open public market. It's a really that's a really big distinction. Um so are are in terms of understanding if you are buying the stock not the IPO but if you're playing in that stock you know in the first few days it's public these companies are going to have to operate now on a different set of rules when they're public right so anything that's been happening in terms of private valuation does that does that automatically carry over into the public market or are they going to have to perform against different benchmarks that may impact the stock perform. >> Yeah, I think they have to perform against different benchmarks. I mean, generally speaking, the prices set in private markets over time, the different rounds of financing are from more sophisticated investors assessing what they think the value is. So, I'd say it's unlikely, maybe not impossible, but unlikely companies choose to go public at anything that was below prior private market, >> you know, valuations. But once you're public, all your financial records are out there. People are looking at more short-term naturatured discussions. Like we mentioned, just SpaceX as an example, it's been in business 24 years, right? So, they've had quarter century to build things and maybe not have the same scrutiny that a a public market would force on them about, well, where are your earnings and what's next quarter going to look like, right? To have a little bit of a longer term >> perspective. and and that's sometimes why companies stay private because they don't want, you know, the the extra burden and scrutiny of of the public markets. But um and we'll see. I mean, SpaceX will be the first one, but as you mentioned, there's a couple other AI companies coming up. So, um each its own company, each different, but we will get a flavor of it. But again, I think the most important takeaway is put all the IPO talk aside. These companies, just based on the valuations that are being thrown around, are likely to be large constituents in index funds. They're going to be large components of the market that could either drive the market higher or pull the market down. Much like we've seen in recent years with this concentration risk of what people named the magnificent seven, you know, the seven tech stocks, we're going to have a similar dynamic that these names are going to have a prevalent waiting just based on their size and all investors are going to have to be aware of that and these will, you know, be drivers in the market going forward. >> Yeah, they're going to be drivers. They're also going to be impacted uh in terms of what happens with the overall market, I would imagine. I mean, we've seen um when fears of geopolitical risk or other, you know, forces outside the fundamentals of any sector take hold, you can see the markets move lower and now if you're publicly traded presumably, um you're vulnerable to that. What I'm I'm interested to hear. So, it sounds like, you know, um there are some risks associated with it and opportunities. I think everyone kind of knows the opportunities. I want to focus a little bit more on the risk. Living up to these lofty valuations certainly sounds like one of them. Um what what do we need to understand about lockups and that type of um selling that sometimes comes around initial public offerings? >> Well, and it's a good point. Most times when an IPO happens, the people that have been private investors along the way, whether employees, the CEO or private investors cannot exit the day of the IPO. There's some type of lock up restriction. Um, typically at 6 months, you know, SpaceX, from what I've seen, has a much more uh graduated schedule over over time based on a number of things. But you know generally speaking there there is going to be a window for every company that comes public where insiders are now given the shares and said if you want if you want to sell them go ahead and you know in theory that can create or does create selling pressure. The question is is there enough demand is there enough buying pressure on the other side of that. So that is something to be aware of. Um >> yeah absolutely I think it's really important. Um, and I feel like we heard a lot about that in the tech boom, but it's been a little while, you know, and so folks who are not sort of super engaged in that technology area. Not that it doesn't happen everywhere else, but it seems especially prevalent because, correct me if I'm wrong, a lot of times in startup culture, you get salary, but a lot of what you're getting is stock because you're sort of investing in the success of this. That's how they're able to get talent. So, you've got some people sitting on um a lot of stock presumably. We've seen the headlines of the potential billionaires that make these >> and a lot of these people remember one other thing especially with more tech oriented companies a lot of these people are young >> you know they might be they might be 27 years old so you know put yourself as a 27y old and someone hands you this money you know uh so you know you might have a different perspective if you're 65 getting that kind of wealth unlocking versus if you're 25 or 40. So, that's the other thing to keep in mind. There's a lot of young wealth tied up in this. Um, but it's exciting um for them and it's it's a reminder, too. I mean, we've talked a lot in our interviews, Maggie, about investing in innovation. So, despite >> the risk of the world, there's a side of thinking that you've got to invest in innovation because the world changes and if you don't change, the world will change around you is a saying I like. And I want to just throw one thing out. I think it's just a good reference point. And that is just look at the S&P 500 alone starting in 1985 and you say well what were the two largest companies 1985 it was IBM and Exxon jump 10 years ahead to 95 what were the two top companies GE and AT&T now let's go to Apple and Exxon let's go to 2025 Apple and Nvidia so now let's jump ahead to 2035 who are those top companies going to be it's you We don't know the answer to that, but it's likely not going to be all of the old uh cohorts from yester years, right? >> Yeah. Well, not not the old guard, the new guard, which is why I think that we're talking about this because there's so much sort of excitement and anticipation and understanding that we may be watching the public debut of companies that are literally going to be at the center of the tech universe. Um, and I, you know, I think that you can disagree on valuations, you can disagree on quarterto quarter, but I think that sense of where the ball is moving, um, is shared by by an awful lot of people. So, let's talk a little bit about So, those are the things you have to consider when you're looking at these these each of these different IPOs on their own. Let's talk a little bit about that broader market impact as well, because people may be sitting here and saying, "That's too rich for me. That's too risky for me. I'm not I'm not going to chase that." But you we still all may feel the impact of this, right? So you mentioned uh getting included really quickly in the index. Um why is that so important? What is that going to trigger if they almost immediately based on their their market cap get included? >> Yeah. Yeah. Well, again, that's part of what's novel this time. You've got very large companies coming public. And I think the indexes historically companies coming public are smaller and they've got to prove themselves and show their trading pattern for a period of time. But I think the indexes are all evaluating a fast track because pretend this were Nvidia. It would seem silly to have Nvidia not in an index if we're that large and big of a company, you know, if that were coming out. So each index, you know, is is coming up with its own rules, but I have seen a number of them advance fasttracking. So it's likely the these names will be included in indexes quicker than in the past. And so again, even if you're a passive investor in your 401k and index funds, thinking these things have nothing to do with you, the reality is these names will end up likely being fairly large constituents in some of these index funds that you are invested in. So this kind of impacts the entire market, which is why I say let's broaden this out beyond just things going public. >> Yeah. So, so if that is true, and since a lot of people use index funds, we will presumably all be shareholders of these companies. Yeah, anybody that's in an index over time will likely will likely own this. And you know the the one problem that always exists with index funds versus active management is their price indiscriminate. Meaning >> they have to buy will buy at any price to get to the right percent level of whatever stock it is. Not just the ones we're talking about, but just how these mechanisms work. Um and so that can have implications as well. And so there will be people that say, well, I'm a little more worried about index funds just buying in at top dollar. I would rather be in funds that uh an active manager is deciding whether something is a good value relative to the price or not. Right. So that's the age-old debate of course of active management versus indexing. >> Yes. And and we and and we know we we have a fondness for active management um and getting people sort of help individually with their portfolios which is why we offer free portfolio reviews. Um what about the impact on other IPOs? So the you know the IPO process is really important part of the you know US capitalist system getting these companies who've hit a certain level the ability to raise more money to continue to grow. Um it's launched some of the most successful companies we've ever seen. Does this the fact that these sort of monster companies are coming to market is there a risk that sucks up all the capital and interest at the expense of other companies? >> Well, that is a debate going on right now right at the size of these and the dollars involved. Um, you know, do they take away from other IPOs in Q coming later this year? Do they take away from other sectors of the stock market? I mean, money is fungeible, right? So if people put more money in certain things, they have to take it out from somewhere. And I don't think any of us know the answer to that other than this does pave the way um for other IPOs. It could be in a positive way. It could be if if these IPOs are successful and do well, more companies decide it's time for them to go public and it kind of lifts all boats. On the other hand, um you know, the they could come out, be overpriced, go down in in value, and it cools the IPO market. So, we've seen that in the past, too. So, um, again, IPOs themselves is they're not something we generally do at that moment because they bring out animal spirits and so much emotion and frenzy and hype that uh that I just think caution's warranted. It's not that it's not that an investor can't decide to invest in these names, but I think they have to balance the opportunity set with some of the risks we threw out of of any stock or investment decision. It's just price relative to upside. And you know, these are new areas. None of us really know what the space or orbital economy, as we've talked about, will become. And we're all just trying to figure out where AI is going on a daily basis as well. So, these are exciting areas, but it's not crystal clear on on where they go and where earnings come from and and how quickly those earnings materialize. That's part of the puzzle here. >> Yeah. Yeah. Which is why we say all the time sort of, you know, have a framework. Don't get married to the narrative and make sure you understand your risk tolerance because there there is risk involved and I know that there's opportunity because you and I talk all the time. You're very keen on the innovation theme, but you always have to look at it um the opportunity balanced with the risk. Um so I'm so glad we were able to sort of do this setup and have this conversation today because I think we're going to be talking about these names certainly all summer. So we want everyone to have some time to sort of think about uh if it's the right fit for them. Appreciate you today, Brett. Great stuff. >> Thanks,
